candidates.title: ChatGPT Chemistry Assistant for Text Mining and the Prediction of MOF Synthesis
Automated Literature Review Using NLP Techniques and LLM-Based Retrieval-Augmented Generation
candidates.abstract: This research presents and compares multiple approaches to automate the generation of literature reviews using several Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with a Large Language Model (LLM). The ever-increasing number of research articles provides a huge challenge for manual literature review. It has resulted in an increased demand for automation. Developing a system capable of automatically generating the literature reviews from only the PDF files as input is the primary objective of this research work. The effectiveness of several Natural Language Processing (NLP) strategies, such as the frequency-based method (spaCy), the transformer model (Simple T5), and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with Large Language Model (GPT-3.5-turbo), is evaluated to meet the primary objective. The SciTLDR dataset is chosen for this research experiment and three distinct techniques are utilized to implement three different systems for auto-generating the literature reviews. The ROUGE scores are used for the evaluation of all three systems. Based on the evaluation, the Large Language Model GPT-3.5-turbo achieved the highest ROUGE-1 score, 0.364. The transformer model comes in second place and spaCy is at the last position. Finally, a graphical user interface is created for the best system based on the large language model.

deduped.canonical_title: Automated Literature Review Using NLP Techniques and LLM-Based Retrieval-Augmented Generation

groups.title: ChatGPT Chemistry Assistant for Text Mining and the Prediction of MOF Synthesis
Automated Literature Review Using NLP Techniques and LLM-Based Retrieval-Augmented Generation

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: The ever-increasing number of research articles makes manual literature review time-consuming, tedious, and error-prone, creating a need for automated systems that can generate literature reviews from PDF inputs.
profiles.domain: Natural Language Processing, Text Summarization, Academic Writing Assistance
profiles.method_summary: Three NLP approaches are implemented and compared: (1) Frequency-based spaCy model using word frequency calculation for sentence extraction, (2) Transformer-based Simple T5 model fine-tuned on SciTLDR dataset, (3) LLM-based GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). All approaches take PDF inputs and generate literature review summaries.
datasets: SciTLDR dataset (5,400 TLDRs from over 3,200 scientific papers from Hugging Face)
tasks: Automated literature review generation from PDF files

main_claims.claim_text: The LLM-based approach (GPT-3.5-turbo) achieved the highest ROUGE-1 score of 0.364, outperforming both T5 (0.268) and spaCy (0.257)
All three NLP approaches (spaCy, T5, and GPT-3.5-TURBO) can produce satisfactory results in automating literature review generation
LLM-based models outperform older NLP approaches (transformer and frequency-based) for automated literature review generation
A functional GUI tool was successfully created using the best-performing LLM-based system where users can upload multiple PDFs to automatically generate coherent literature reviews

limitations.text: The model is not yet fully capable of completely automating literature reviews and still requires human reviewers for validation
The system was only evaluated on the SciTLDR dataset which may not represent all scientific domains

future_work: Add more functionality to GUI such as model options and output size controls
Explore additional models including BERT, Gemini, and LLaMA for potentially better results
Enhance system effectiveness and applicability across diverse domains
profiles.notes: The research presents three end-to-end pipelines for literature review automation, allowing users to generate literature reviews directly from PDF inputs. The LLM approach uses RAG with GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 as the backend. The T5 model was fine-tuned on SciTLDR while spaCy used extractive summarization based on word frequency. Evaluation was performed using ROUGE metrics on test data.

contradictions.reason: Multiple claim types detected under the same task; manual review recommended.

claims.claim_text: The LLM-based approach (GPT-3.5-turbo) achieved the highest ROUGE-1 score of 0.364, outperforming both T5 (0.268) and spaCy (0.257)
All three NLP approaches (spaCy, T5, and GPT-3.5-TURBO) can produce satisfactory results in automating literature review generation
LLM-based models outperform older NLP approaches (transformer and frequency-based) for automated literature review generation
A functional GUI tool was successfully created using the best-performing LLM-based system where users can upload multiple PDFs to automatically generate coherent literature reviews

gaps.gap_statement: Evaluation is limited to the SciTLDR dataset (5,400 TLDRs from 3,200 papers), which may not represent the full spectrum of scientific domains or document structures encountered in real-world literature reviews.
All approaches are evaluated exclusively on English scientific documents, leaving a critical gap in multi-lingual literature review automation.
Only three specific NLP approaches (spaCy extractive, T5 abstractive, GPT-3.5 with RAG) are compared, representing a narrow slice of available text summarization methods.
Evaluation relies solely on ROUGE metrics (ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L, ROUGE-Lsum), which may not capture semantic quality, factual consistency, or task-specific utility for literature reviews.

The paper conflates single-document TLDR generation with multi-document literature review synthesis, but does not explicitly evaluate the more complex task of integrating multiple papers into coherent thematic summaries.
The system acknowledges needing human reviewers for validation but provides no framework for interactive refinement or user feedback integration.

steps.name: writing

outline.title: Introduction: Automated Literature Review Generation
outline.objective: Contextualize the problem of manual literature review bottleneck and introduce the motivation for automated text summarization approaches in scientific document processing

outline.title: NLP Approaches for Literature Review Automation
outline.objective: Present and compare the three implemented methodologies: extractive (spaCy frequency-based), abstractive (T5 fine-tuned), and LLM-based (GPT-3.5 with RAG) approaches

outline.title: Evaluation Framework and Dataset Limitations
outline.objective: Analyze the reliance on SciTLDR dataset and ROUGE metrics, examining how single-dataset evaluation constrains generalizability claims

outline.title: Single-Document vs. Multi-Document Summarization
outline.objective: Examine the distinction between TLDR generation (single-document) and literature review synthesis (multi-document integration), addressing the methodological conflation in the evaluated approach

outline.title: Human-AI Collaboration and Interactive Refinement
outline.objective: Discuss the absence of frameworks for user feedback integration and interactive refinement in the current literature review automation pipeline

outline.title: Multilingual and Cross-Domain Generalization
outline.objective: Address the critical gap in evaluating approaches across non-English scientific documents and diverse research domains

sections.title: Introduction: Automated Literature Review Generation
sections.text: This review synthesizes evidence from 1 papers addressing Multi-document summarization for academic papers; Automated literature review generation from PDF files, drawn from the automated literature review domain. Systems examined employ diverse methodological families, including Text Summarization (Extractive and Abstractive). The review structures findings around 4 discrete claims, organized into a comparative matrix that highlights where methods converge, diverge, or lack sufficient evaluation evidence. The objective is to move beyond siloed paper summaries toward a structured understanding of the current state, open problems, and underexplored regions of the literature.

sections.title: NLP Approaches for Literature Review Automation
sections.text: Text Summarization (Extractive and Abstractive) systems make claims including: The LLM-based approach (GPT-3.5-turbo) achieved the highest ROUGE-1 score of 0.364, outperforming both T5 (0.268) and spaCy (0.257); All three NLP approaches (spaCy, T5, and GPT-3.5-TURBO) can produce satisfactory results in automating literature review generation.

sections.title: Evaluation Framework and Dataset Limitations
sections.text: Reported metrics include: ROUGE-1; ROUGE-2; ROUGE-L; ROUGE-Lsum; ROUGE-1; ROUGE-2; ROUGE-L; ROUGE-Lsum; ROUGE-1; ROUGE-2; ROUGE-L; ROUGE-Lsum; ROUGE-1; ROUGE-2; ROUGE-L; ROUGE-Lsum. Metric reporting practices vary considerably across papers, making direct cross-study comparison difficult in several cases.

sections.title: Single-Document vs. Multi-Document Summarization
sections.text: This review has examined 1 papers covering Multi-document summarization for academic papers; Automated literature review generation from PDF files using Text Summarization (Extractive and Abstractive). Key gaps identified include: [gap-1] Evaluation is limited to the SciTLDR dataset (5,400 TLDRs from 3,200 papers), which may not represent the full spectrum of scientific domains or document structures encountered in real-world literature reviews. [gap-2] All approaches are evaluated exclusively on English scientific documents, leaving a critical gap in multi-lingual literature review automation. [gap-3] Only three specific NLP approaches (spaCy extractive, T5 abstractive, GPT-3.5 with RAG) are compared, representing a narrow slice of available text summarization methods.. Future research directions emerging from this analysis include addressing metric standardization, expanding linguistic coverage, and resolving conflicting performance claims through rigorous comparative evaluation.

sections.title: Human-AI Collaboration and Interactive Refinement
sections.text: This section synthesizes findings from 1 papers in the review corpus, addressing Multi-document summarization for academic papers; Automated literature review generation from PDF files with Text Summarization (Extractive and Abstractive). The comparative evidence matrix structures claims, metrics, and limitations to support structured analysis.

sections.title: Multilingual and Cross-Domain Generalization
sections.text: This section synthesizes findings from 1 papers in the review corpus, addressing Multi-document summarization for academic papers; Automated literature review generation from PDF files with Text Summarization (Extractive and Abstractive). The comparative evidence matrix structures claims, metrics, and limitations to support structured analysis.
